top_requirements.requirement: Observability/monitoring tooling (Prometheus, Grafana, Datadog, or general "observability")

top_requirements.approx_frequency: 237 of 414 named Terraform specifically (~57%); 171/414 (~41%) used generic "IaC" language

top_requirements.requirement: Multi-cloud exposure beyond AWS (GCP and/or Azure)

top_requirements.approx_frequency: 106/414 (~26%) named on-call; 98/414 (~24%) named incident response — a real minority, not universal

top_requirements.requirement: AI/ML infrastructure fluency (model serving, training infra, GPU clusters, LLM platforms)
top_requirements.approx_frequency: 133/414 (~32%) mention AI/ML/GPU/LLM in body text; 45/414 (~11%) have AI/ML explicitly in the job title

top_requirements.requirement: Cross-functional collaboration / stakeholder management named explicitly

top_requirements.approx_frequency: 325/414 (~79%) state an explicit years figure, typically 5+ for senior, 8-10+ for staff/principal
only 47/414 (~11%) explicitly gate on a bachelor's degree — most postings are experience-gated, not degree-gated

top_responsibilities: Design, build, and operate shared cloud infrastructure (compute, networking, storage, Kubernetes clusters) that multiple product/engineering teams build on top of, rather than infra scoped to a single team.
Own and evolve CI/CD pipelines and infrastructure-as-code (Terraform/Pulumi-style) so other engineers can provision and deploy without deep infra expertise.
Improve observability/monitoring and drive down toil — instrumenting systems, tuning alerting, and reducing manual operational work.
Participate in an on-call rotation and respond to incidents affecting shared infrastructure, though this is a substantial minority pattern (~a quarter of postings) rather than the majority framing.
At senior/staff level, drive technical strategy/architecture decisions with cross-org scope, evaluate build-vs-buy and platform consolidation choices, and mentor other engineers.

A large and fast-growing subset (roughly a third of the corpus) center specifically on AI/ML infrastructure — GPU cluster management, model training/serving platforms, and inference infra — rather than general-purpose developer platforms.

Collaborate with product engineering teams on adoption of internal tooling and golden paths, though explicit "internal customer"/"platform as a product" framing is rare in the actual posting text compared to how prominently the existing brief foregrounds it.
Cost/efficiency optimization of cloud spend and shared infrastructure footprint comes up as a recurring but secondary responsibility theme.

comp_signals.range_text: Senior Staff+ Infrastructure Engineer, Cluster Infrastructure — Annual Salary: $405,000 – $485,000 USD
Senior Staff Machine Learning Platform Engineer — California pay range $295,000 to $405,500
Research Platform Engineer — Pay Range $200k-$300k base salary (SF Bay Area estimate)
Senior Platform Engineer II, Compute Services — base salary range $165,000 to $242,000
DevOps Engineer / Forward Deployed Infrastructure Engineer - US Government — estimated salary range $135,000 - $200,000/year
Platform Engineer - Kubernetes — typical starting salary range $94,300 – $149,200 USD
Advanced Information Technologist – Associate Cloud Engineer — Associate (Level 2) pay range $97,750 - $132,250
Associate DevOps Engineer — Base Pay Range $71,000 - $80,000 (lowest observed in the corpus)

seniority_distribution_notes: Heavily senior-skewed: 184/414 (~44%) titles contain "Senior," another 45/414 (~11%) are "Staff," and 18/414 (~4%) are "Principal" — roughly 60% of the corpus is senior-or-above by title alone. Only 4/414 titles read as junior/entry/associate and 3/414 are internships. The remaining ~38% (159/414) carry no seniority qualifier, which in practice reads as mid-level (IC3-ish) rather than true entry-level — title text and years-of-experience gates (5+ years typical, 8-10+ for staff/principal) both confirm this skews senior. This matches the existing brief's framing that junior/entry direct-hire is possible but atypical.
notable_divergence_from_existing_brief: Two things stand out. (1) The brief leans heavily on "platform as a product" / "internal customer" framing to justify a high stakeholder_client_comfort weight (0.9, second-highest in the scorecard) and a high ambiguity_tolerance target. In the actual posting text that specific framing is rare: literal "internal customer" appears in only 3/414 postings, "platform as a product"/"product mindset" phrasing in only 4/414, and even generic "requirements gathering" language in just 5/414. "Stakeholder" (95/414, ~23%) and "cross-functional" (106/414, ~26%) are present but describe a real-but-modest minority pattern, not a defining majority theme in the raw text — the brief's narrative is directionally right (this is genuinely more stakeholder-facing than a pure ops role) but the postings themselves undersell it rhetorically compared to how strongly the brief argues it, likely because postings favor generic engineering-requirements boilerplate over articulating the product-mindset framing that industry commentary (Team Topologies, The New Stack, etc.) uses. (2) The brief treats AI-infra-adjacent platform work as a comp premium footnote, but in this fresh 2026 sample it looks more like a structural shift: ~32% of postings (133/414) reference AI/ML/GPU/LLM infrastructure in the body and 11% (45/414) have AI/ML explicitly in the title, with several of the highest observed comp figures in the whole corpus (Anthropic $405K-$485K, Faire $295K-$405K, Hadrian $250K-$320K, World Labs $200K-$300K) attached specifically to ML/AI-platform-flavored titles rather than generic platform engineering. The existing dimension_evidence for ml_engineering_fluency (target=1, weight=0.1, rationale \"not relevant to this archetype's scope\") is worth a second look given how large and well-compensated this AI-infra subset now is — though the target is likely still directionally correct since these roles need ML-systems operational fluency (serving/training infra), not ML modeling skill itself, which is what that dimension actually measures.

dimension_evidence.note: Direct textual evidence is thin (explicit "requirements gathering" language appears in only 5/414 postings), but the corpus's strong senior/staff skew (60%+ of titles) and frequent "drive strategy"/"own the roadmap"/"define best practices" language in those postings is consistent with a role that expects self-directed scope definition rather than ticket-driven work. Supports the target directionally but not with strong direct quotes.

On-call named in 106/414 (~26%) and incident response in 98/414 (~24%) — a real but clear minority. A mid-scale target (3, not 4-5) matches a role where interrupt-driven work is present but not the norm for most postings in the sample.

Same on-call/incident-response figures (~24-26% of postings) support a moderate rather than low or high target — consistent with the brief's note that Platform Engineer and SRE blend at smaller companies without on-call being the defining mandate.

No posting-text proxy directly measures diagnostic depth, but the same ~24% incident-response subset implies real live-debugging demand for a meaningful minority of roles, consistent with a moderate (not top-end) target across the full archetype.

Strongly supported: "architecture/architect" language appears in 263/414 (~64%) and scalability language in 243/414 (~59%) of postings — consistent with this being the archetype's single highest-weighted, defining trait.

Postings commonly stack Kubernetes (61%), Terraform/IaC (57%/41%), and multi-cloud (AWS 64%, GCP 35%, Azure 31%) requirements simultaneously, matching the brief's "2-3 adjacent infra layers" framing rather than single-tool depth or extreme breadth.

The brief's specific adoption/DevEx-metric framing (self-service, DevEx language at 13%, explicit IDP language at only 4%) is present but modest in raw posting text, which fits a moderate rather than extreme accountability target — real but not as sharply articulated as the brief's prose suggests.

This is the dimension with the clearest gap between brief narrative and raw posting evidence: literal internal-customer/product-mindset language is nearly absent (3/414 and 4/414 respectively), and even general stakeholder/cross-functional language sits at only ~23-26% of postings. The underlying claim (this role is more stakeholder-facing than a pure infra-code job) is plausible and matches industry commentary cited in the brief, but the postings themselves don't provide strong direct support for weighting this as the second-highest dimension in the entire scorecard — worth a closer look at whether 0.9 is calibrated to industry-commentary framing rather than actual JD text.

Mentoring language appears in 128/414 (~31%) postings, mostly senior/staff roles listing "mentor junior engineers" as one bullet among many — consistent with a low-modest target since it's peer mentoring, not public-facing or systematic teaching.
No postings in the sample describe a named external account/customer portfolio structure — consistent with a low target and the low weight assigned.
Same as above — no evidence of durable named external relationships in the sample; internal-team collaboration described (where present) doesn't read as a relationship-continuity axis.

All 68 explicit comp figures found in the corpus describe base salary (plus RSU/equity in several cases, e.g. Palantir, CoreWeave, Anthropic) with no sales-style bonus/commission structure anywhere — strongly supports the target=1.

Python (59%) and Go (42%) are both very commonly required as hands-on languages, and many postings (e.g. Fivetran, ClickHouse, WorkOS) frame the role as writing production libraries/services, not just config — supports a high (not maximal) coding-intensity target.

"Travel" appears in only 58/414 (~14%) postings and "onsite/embed with customer" language essentially never (0 direct hits) except implicitly at Palantir's Forward Deployed roles — those are a small named exception, not the pattern. Supports a low target overall.

Naive "security" keyword hits are high (275/414, ~66%), though this is a broad match that includes generic access-control, secrets-management, and compliance boilerplate rather than adversarial threat-modeling specifically. Worth a manual spot-check pass before concluding the target should move, but the raw frequency is high enough that it's not simply "not a named focus" as the current rationale states.

Data center/hardware language appears in 169/414 (~41%, often generic "data center" as a cloud-region reference) but genuine physical/hardware-constraint framing (e.g. GPU cluster hardware) is narrow — only 4/414 mention GPU clusters specifically. Supports a low target with AI-infra/data-center roles as a small named exception.

Almost no management titles in the sample (1/414 Director/Head-of, 0 EM titles) — corpus is IC-centric, matching a low-modest target despite the fairly common mentoring bullet (31%) that hints at the EM lateral-move pattern described in the brief's career-ladder section.
No postings in the sample describe public speaking, content creation, or external-facing visibility expectations — consistent with a low target for an internal-facing role.

AI/ML/GPU/LLM language appears in 133/414 (~32%) of postings and 45/414 (~11%) of titles explicitly name AI/ML, with several of the highest comp figures in the corpus attached to these roles (Anthropic, Faire, Hadrian, World Labs). The current low target/weight is likely still directionally right since these roles need ML-systems operational fluency (serving/training infra) rather than ML modeling skill (which is what this dimension measures), but the sheer volume and comp premium of this subset is large enough that a reviewer should confirm the target isn't underselling a real and growing slice of this archetype.
No mobile-platform-specific language found anywhere in the sample — fully consistent with the low target.

SQL/database language appears in 113/414 (~27%) and Kafka specifically in 43/414 (~10%) — a real but modest adjacency, consistent with a low-modest target that acknowledges overlap without treating this as a data-engineering archetype.

Overwhelmingly supported: AWS in 64%, Kubernetes in 61%, Terraform in 57%, GCP in 35%, Azure in 31% of the full 414-posting sample — this is unambiguously the literal, hands-on core requirement across the corpus, justifying the maximal target and weight.
